title: The Iran Deal Comes at a Cost to Israel. The White House Hasn’t Acknowledged It. | Council on Foreign Relations
gist: Former Bush/Trump official Elliott Abrams argues the new U.S.-Iran MOU sidelined Israel, handed Tehran sanctions relief and a deconfliction role over Lebanon while deferring Iran's nuclear and missile programs, and that Washington has failed the consultation and public-messaging discipline a sidelined ally requires.

themes.theme: MOU structure favors Iran over Israel

insights.claim: The U.S.-Iran memorandum of understanding mentions Lebanon three times in its first paragraph and declares the immediate and permanent termination of military operations on all fronts including Lebanon, without mentioning Israel at all.
insights.speaker: Elliott Abrams
insights.so_what: Textual exclusion of Israel from a deal governing its active war front signals reduced U.S. backing for Israeli freedom of action against Hezbollah.
insights.quote: the immediate and permanent termination of military operations on all fronts, including in Lebanon
insights.locator: The memorandum of understanding (MOU) between the United States and Iran

insights.claim: A new Lebanon deconfliction mechanism announced under the MOU includes the United States, Iran, Pakistan, and Qatar but excludes Israel, the country actively at war with Hezbollah.
insights.speaker: Elliott Abrams
insights.so_what: Embedding Iran and two Israel-critics into Lebanon's security architecture institutionalizes Iranian influence over a contested border, raising re-escalation risk.

insights.claim: The MOU lifts all U.S. sanctions on Iran, giving Tehran immediate and important financial relief.
insights.speaker: Elliott Abrams
insights.so_what: Full sanctions removal is a near-term macro catalyst for Iranian oil exports and regional capital flows; watch crude supply and Iran-exposed counterparties.
insights.locator: the lifting of all U.S. sanctions on Iran

insights.claim: The MOU contains no mention of Iran's missile program or its support for proxies like Hezbollah, and postpones serious discussion of Iran's nuclear weapons program to later negotiations.
insights.speaker: Elliott Abrams
insights.so_what: Deferring nuclear and missile issues means the deal resolves none of the core proliferation risks, keeping a tail risk of renewed conflict alive.
insights.locator: the absence of any mention of the Iranian missile program

insights.claim: Israel views the linking of Lebanon's internal affairs and national sovereignty to Iran as a great Iranian victory and an incomprehensible U.S. concession.
insights.speaker: Elliott Abrams
insights.so_what: An ally publicly reading a U.S. deal as a strategic loss signals friction that can spill into security, intelligence, and procurement cooperation.
insights.quote: Israel views the linking of Lebanon’s internal affairs and national sovereignty to Iran as a great Iranian victory and an incomprehensible U.S. concession.
insights.locator: Israel views the linking of Lebanon’s internal affairs

themes.theme: Israeli domestic politics and the coming election

insights.claim: Israel will hold an election in September or October 2026 in which voters decide whether Benjamin Netanyahu continues as prime minister or is replaced by an opposition leader.
insights.speaker: Elliott Abrams
insights.so_what: A near-term Israeli election fought partly over the Iran deal injects political risk into Israeli policy continuity and U.S.-Israel coordination.
insights.locator: Israel is now entering an election season

insights.claim: A recent poll found 92.1 percent of Israelis, including Jews and Arabs, agree that Iran gained the most from the MOU, and 86 percent hold a negative view of the agreement.
insights.speaker: Elliott Abrams
insights.so_what: Near-unanimous Israeli disapproval constrains any Israeli government from cooperating with the deal, raising the odds of unilateral Israeli action.
insights.quote: 92.1 percent of Israelis, including Jews and Arabs, agree that Iran gained the most from the MOU
insights.locator: A recent poll indicates that 92.1 percent

insights.claim: Despite a contentious election, Israelis are united on the conflict with Iran and the way it ended, making discontent over the MOU a cross-partisan factor.
insights.speaker: Elliott Abrams
insights.so_what: Cross-partisan consensus means a change of Israeli government would not soften opposition to the deal.
insights.locator: Israelis are now united on a critical issue

insights.claim: Netanyahu has been careful in his public language while his far-right coalition ministers Bezalel Smotrich and Itamar Ben Gvir have not, exposing internal coalition strain over the deal.
insights.speaker: Elliott Abrams
insights.so_what: Far-right minister rhetoric can escalate friction unilaterally, a wildcard for de-escalation and for coalition stability.
insights.locator: some other ministers (unsurprisingly, the two ministers on the far right

themes.theme: Shift in U.S. rhetoric toward Israel

insights.claim: U.S. Secretary of War Pete Hegseth on March 4 called Israel a steadfast partner praising extraordinary cooperation, a markedly warmer tone than the administration now takes.
insights.speaker: Elliott Abrams
insights.so_what: The contrast documents a concrete cooling in U.S.-Israel rhetoric within months, a marker of shifting alignment.
insights.quote: Our ally Israel is demonstrating tremendous capabilities. Extraordinary cooperation with such an ally is amazing and necessary.
insights.locator: U.S. Secretary of War Pete Hegseth called Israel a “steadfast partner”

insights.claim: Vice President JD Vance, the U.S. chief negotiator, told Israeli critics of Trump and the MOU to wake up and smell the reality of the situation, signaling browbeating rather than reassurance of an ally.
insights.speaker: Elliott Abrams
insights.so_what: Public pressure on an ally over its own security deal raises the political cost of cooperation and signals U.S.-Israel divergence.
insights.quote: wake up and smell the reality of the situation that country is in
insights.locator: Vice President JD Vance, who suggested that Israeli critics

themes.theme: Allies' divergence after a shared war

insights.claim: Abrams frames the U.S.-Israel rift through the WWII U.S.-UK analogy: closest allies during war diverge sharply on peace terms once interests overlap less after the fighting ends.
insights.speaker: Elliott Abrams
insights.so_what: A historical lens that predicts post-conflict allied friction is structural, not a one-off, useful for anticipating further divergence.
insights.locator: During World War II, the United States and the United Kingdom

insights.claim: Abrams argues the U.S.-Israel gap is narrower than it appears because both oppose Iran's nuclear program, both want a sovereign Lebanon free of Hezbollah, and both seek to prevent Hamas from regaining control of Gaza.
insights.speaker: Elliott Abrams
insights.so_what: Underlying goal alignment caps the downside of the rift, suggesting friction is tactical and reversible rather than a strategic rupture.
insights.quote: despite the policy differences, both countries’ underlying goals remain aligned

insights.claim: Keeping Israel out of negotiations gives the U.S. tactical flexibility and leaves Israel freer to act unilaterally, consistent with an ask-forgiveness-not-permission approach between the two.
insights.speaker: Elliott Abrams
insights.so_what: If both sides reserve unilateral action, the probability of unannounced Israeli strikes on Hezbollah or Iran stays elevated.
insights.locator: It’s clear that the United States gains some tactical flexibility

insights.claim: While Vance discussed Lebanon with Iran, Qatar, and Pakistan in Switzerland, Israeli and Lebanese diplomats met separately in Washington under U.S. auspices, with Israel unsure how the two tracks relate.
insights.speaker: Elliott Abrams
insights.so_what: Two uncoordinated parallel Lebanon tracks create policy ambiguity that an ally is publicly flagging, a sign of process breakdown.
insights.locator: While the vice president was in Switzerland discussing Lebanon

insights.claim: Israel's ambassador in Washington said he needed clarity about U.S. policy and the new deconfliction mechanism, stating Iran's role is to get out of Lebanon, not to restrain Hezbollah.
insights.speaker: Elliott Abrams
insights.so_what: An ambassador publicly demanding clarity signals an alliance operating with insufficient private explanation, a leading indicator of further friction.
insights.quote: The basic premise [of the talks in Washington] was that Iran was not involved
insights.locator: Its ambassador in Washington said that he needed “clarity”

insights.claim: Abrams concludes that sidelining an ally can be useful but always requires the U.S. to consult, weigh the ally's interests, explain its thinking, and use public language that maintains confidence rather than deepens differences.
insights.speaker: Elliott Abrams
insights.so_what: A normative checklist for managing sidelined allies that the author says Washington is currently failing, predicting continued strain.
insights.quote: The United States has some catching up to do in this case.
insights.locator: Sidelining an ally in negotiations affecting its vital interests
